rationales.stimulation: He is pulled toward "the feeling of standing at the edge of something that does not have a guaranteed outcome," showing attraction to uncertainty and challenge.
rationales.security: He rejects the "smart move" of parking capital and keeping reserves stable, showing willingness to disturb a secure position.

rationales.stimulation: He is already "half-committed" and knows he may be "chasing the feeling again," which points to active attraction to risk and novelty.
rationales.security: He runs "downside scenarios" and frames the worst case as "recoverable," showing deliberate attention to limiting risk before acting.

rationales.benevolence: He says yes to the summer trip with Ingrid and values the part of himself that wanted to "just walk along the water with Ingrid without calculating anything," prioritizing closeness in the relationship.

rationales.stimulation: He explicitly chooses not to chase "the feeling of the leap" and says he does not need to "blow that up to feel alive," rejecting uncertainty-seeking.
rationales.security: He passes on the deal because it is "the responsible move," emphasizing reserves, portfolio stability, and being in "a solid rhythm."

rationales.achievement: He spends "the entire day on calls, restructuring scenarios" and gets to "a workable plan," showing focused competence under pressure.
rationales.security: He says the ground shifted after he had "gotten comfortable with the idea of things being stable," and now instability feels like a threat.

rationales.achievement: He spends days on-site finding a replacement client and says he enjoys "the satisfaction of fixing what is broken," showing effective problem-solving.
rationales.benevolence: He goes to Antwerp in person because "the founder needed to see someone calm in the room," showing support for a close working relationship.

rationales.stimulation: He says "good shape feels like treading water" and keeps checking for something that will make tomorrow "feel different from today," showing restlessness with stability.

rationales.hedonism: He describes the Kinderdijk outing as "exactly what I needed" and enjoys hours walking and eating pannenkoeken, centering present enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: He takes the trip with Ingrid and leaves his phone behind, showing attentive time with someone close rather than work distraction.

rationales.stimulation: He stays up late building a financial model at "one in the morning" because the deal is "exactly the kind of thing I do when something grabs me," showing excitement for a new challenge.
rationales.security: He closes the laptop because he does not want to "stretch us thin again" and reminds himself that "the reserves are there for a reason," prioritizing protection over impulse.

rationales.stimulation: He admits "I miss the feeling of building something from nothing," showing a clear pull toward novelty and high-intensity challenge.
rationales.security: He knows he "swore I would not overextend" after Antwerp but is tempted to ignore that caution because the deck feels exciting.
He asks for "two weeks to think" and reflects on finding "a speed that works for both," showing an effort to balance ambition with protection rather than rush in.

rationales.hedonism: He says "my eyes hurt and I skipped lunch entirely," sacrificing basic comfort and enjoyment for work.
rationales.achievement: He spends hours fixing an operations failure by writing "a detailed process document" and doing both his work and Camilo's to keep things running.
He frames accelerator acceptance as "the first external proof that what we're doing is real," emphasizing recognition and validation of performance.
He tracks that retention was "the second highest in the room," checks everyone's slides, and says "everything is a ranking," showing strong focus on excelling against external benchmarks.
He highlights that revenue is up 14% and CAC is down, celebrating concrete performance gains on the business metrics he tracks.

rationales.hedonism: He skips brunch and then checks email "under the table" during lunch, showing present enjoyment is subordinated to work.
rationales.achievement: He insists the accelerator, pitch comp, and launch are all "steps" in what he is building, clearly prioritizing advancement and performance.
rationales.benevolence: He dismisses Laura's frustration about always coming second to work, showing reduced responsiveness to a close partner's needs.

rationales.achievement: He does not want to be merely in a "strong position" and wants to be "the one they point to," showing a strong drive to stand out for excellence.
rationales.power: He says that standout status would create "leverage" and change investor conversations before he pitches, focusing on influence and reputation.

rationales.achievement: He treats the pitch competition as a legitimate reason to miss the wedding, placing venture goals ahead of family obligations.
rationales.tradition: Skipping his cousin's wedding and disappointing his mother shows a willingness to set aside an important family ritual.
rationales.benevolence: He misses a close family member's wedding and notes his tia and mother were disappointed, showing harm to close relationships for work.

rationales.achievement: He fixates on placing "Second. Out of sixty companies" and the judges calling them "the most polished," emphasizing competitive success and recognition.

rationales.hedonism: He has been working from "6 AM" to almost midnight and Laura sends dinner because she knows he "wouldn't eat otherwise," showing self-denial for work.
rationales.achievement: He calls the investor meetings "what all the prep was for" and links every late night to the chance to move from bootstrapped to funded.
rationales.power: He says funding changes how "suppliers negotiate" and whether hires return calls, showing concern with the influence and status money brings.

rationales.achievement: His frustration centers on "I did everything right and it still wasn't enough," showing strong identification with performance, competence, and results.
He presents the signed term sheet as proof he stayed "focused" and kept pushing through the setback, showing strong performance orientation.
rationales.security: He admits he was running scenarios about having to "go back to bootstrapping for another year" and felt his "shoulders drop" when that threat was removed.
